lesson.menu_desc: Describe charts and figures: trend verbs (rise, fall, peak, plateau) with adverbs (sharply, steadily) and prepositions of change (rose to, fell by, from X to Y) -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 -- Data-Driven -- Describing Trends and Numbers
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Maria Claudia Curimbaba | Aula 17 -- Data-Driven

listenings.text: Good morning. Let me walk you through the quarterly results before the call with our partner. Start with revenue. Sales rose sharply in the first quarter, from forty million to fifty-two million reais. Then growth slowed: in the second quarter the figure increased only slightly, by about two percent. In the third quarter sales reached a peak of fifty-eight million, the highest point of the year. After that, demand cooled and the line dipped a little, falling by three million in the fourth quarter. Costs tell a different story. They climbed gradually all year, a steady rise of about one percent per month, with no sudden jumps. The exchange rate, on the other hand, fluctuated a lot; it moved up and down week after week with no clear direction. So the headline is simple: revenue surged early, plateaued in the middle, and then dipped, while costs rose steadily. When you describe this to the partner, match the verb to the shape of the line.

Before I present these numbers to David, I want my language to be precise. The shape of the line should choose the verb. When sales jump up fast, I will say they surged or rose sharply. When they drop fast, I will say they plummeted or fell sharply. When the line is almost flat, I will say it plateaued or remained steady. When it goes up and down with no pattern, I will say it fluctuated. I also need the right preposition. I say sales rose to fifty-eight million when I give the final figure, but sales rose by six million when I give the difference, and revenue grew from forty to fifty-two million when I give both ends. And I must control the adverbs: a sharp rise is fast and big, a gradual rise is slow and smooth, and a slight rise is very small. If I match the verb, the adverb, and the preposition to the chart, the story will be clear and the partner will trust my reading of the data.

extra_audio.text: First, you read the title and the axes, so you know what the chart measures and over what period. Then, you find the overall trend: did the line rise, fall, or stay steady across the whole period? After that, you describe the biggest movements with strong verbs, a sharp surge or a steep drop. Next, you point out the peak and the lowest point, the highest and the lowest figures. Finally, you add the exact numbers with the right preposition, rose to, fell by, or grew from one figure to another, and you summarize the story in one sentence.
